poor.text: Mate, this room is working against you harder than it should be. The walls are basically a second band you didn't hire.
poor.mateAdvice: Start with speaker placement — get them away from the front wall and you'll hear a difference immediately.

poor.text: Look, it's not great right now. Your gear deserves better than what this room is doing to it.
poor.mateAdvice: Don't buy anything new until you've sorted the room — treatment will get you further than new speakers at this point.

poor.text: The room is the problem here, not the kit. You could drop ten grand on new cables and it wouldn't help.
poor.mateAdvice: Bass traps in the corners are your best first investment — they'll clean up half these issues on their own.

poor.text: Honestly, this room is rough. But every single issue here is fixable, and you know exactly where to start.
poor.mateAdvice: Have a look at the Simulate page — it'll show you exactly what treatment gets you the biggest improvement for the least money.

okay.text: Not bad, actually. There's definitely stuff going on, but nothing here is catastrophic.
okay.mateAdvice: A bit of acoustic panel work and some speaker placement tweaks should get this sounding considerably better.

okay.text: It's workable. You can still hear the music for what it is, but there's definitely room to breathe here.
okay.mateAdvice: Try moving the listening position away from the back wall — even 30cm can smooth out the bass response noticeably.

okay.text: Decent foundation. The room's not killing you, but it's not helping you either.
okay.mateAdvice: First-reflection panels on the side walls would be the biggest bang for your buck right now.

okay.text: You're in the ballpark. A few targeted tweaks and this could genuinely surprise you.
okay.mateAdvice: Check the individual metrics — the worst one is usually the easiest to fix.

good.text: Good room, this. You've clearly put some thought into it.
good.mateAdvice: The remaining issues are minor — small placement adjustments or targeted treatment will close most of the gap.

good.text: Solid result. You're past the point where the room is the obvious problem.
This is genuinely good. Most people never get their rooms to this level.
good.mateAdvice: If you want to push further, the Simulate page can show you what incremental treatment changes would actually do.

good.text: Nice work. The foundations are solid and the remaining gaps are small.

excellent.text: Honestly? Stop messing with the settings and just enjoy the music. You nailed it.
This is about as good as it gets in a domestic room. Outstanding result.
The room is transparent. You're hearing the music, not the walls — that's the whole game.
Seriously impressive. Most pro studios don't measure this well. Go put a record on.

poor.text: You've got a massive dip in the mid-bass — classic SBIR dead spot. Your speakers and the front wall are literally fighting each other.
poor.mateAdvice: Pulling the speakers away from the front wall by even 20cm often kills that null stone dead — try it before anything else.

poor.text: The bass is all over the place. Boomy in some spots, completely absent in others. You're hearing the room modes more than the music.
poor.mateAdvice: Try shifting the listening position forward — even half a metre can move you off the worst resonance node.

poor.text: Big peaks and dips in the low end. The room's standing waves are carving chunks out of your bass response.
poor.mateAdvice: Bass traps in all four corners absorb the worst mode energy — try to get them as tall as possible.

poor.text: It's a modal mess below Schroeder. Certain bass notes are going to sound huge while others just disappear.
poor.mateAdvice: Speaker and listening position are the free fixes — try the 38% rule for placement before spending on treatment.

okay.text: A few lumps in the bass region. Nothing catastrophic, but you'll notice certain notes sounding fatter than others.
okay.mateAdvice: A bit more front-wall distance on the speakers would likely smooth at least one of those bumps.

okay.text: Some modal activity below Schroeder, but it's manageable. The sweet spot might just be slightly off where you're sitting.
okay.mateAdvice: Try sliding the listening position forward or back by about 30–40cm and re-measure.

okay.text: The response isn't flat but it's not a disaster. A few bass notes are going to punch harder than others.
okay.mateAdvice: Targeted bass trapping at the front corners specifically would address what's left here.

okay.text: Modal behaviour's present but in check. You'll get further with placement experiments than reaching straight for treatment.
okay.mateAdvice: Toe the speakers in slightly more — it can shift the first-reflection points and often helps the modal response too.

good.text: Reasonably flat response — the modes are there but well-behaved. Nice.
Good frequency balance. A few minor lumps left in the bass but nothing that should bother you much.
good.mateAdvice: If you want to chase the last couple of dB of flatness, some corner treatment would likely get you there.

good.text: The bass isn't perfectly flat but it's honest and even. You're past the obvious problems.
Solid result. The room modes are controlled enough that you can trust what you're hearing in the low end.

excellent.text: Practically flat through the bass. Whatever you've done with placement and treatment, it's working.
Textbook response. The modal region is tightly controlled — you're hearing the recording, not the room.
Impressively even. No dead spots, no massive humps. This is what you're aiming for.
Near-reference flatness. The modes exist but they're not running the show anymore.

poor.text: The early reflections are all over the place. That phantom center you're supposed to hear? It's getting smeared across the room.
poor.mateAdvice: A couple of absorptive panels at the first-reflection points on the side walls would transform the imaging immediately.

poor.text: Reflections are arriving too early and too loud. The stereo image is going to feel vague and unfocused.
poor.mateAdvice: Sit closer to the speakers if you can — reduces the time gap between direct and reflected sound significantly.

poor.text: It's like listening through a fog. The side walls are throwing reflected copies of everything straight back at you.
poor.mateAdvice: Side-wall panels at ear height are the most impactful single treatment you can add in this room.

poor.text: The reflections are competing with the direct sound. You're not hearing the stereo image — you're hearing the room's messy version of it.
poor.mateAdvice: Even some thick blankets or a bookshelf on the side walls will immediately show you what you've been missing.

okay.text: Reflections are present and you'll notice them on wide stereo sources. Not catastrophic but definitely improvable.
okay.mateAdvice: Sit in your listening seat and have a mate hold a mirror along the side wall — where you see the tweeter is where the panel goes.

okay.text: Some reflected energy making it to your ears, but the phantom center is just about holding together.
okay.mateAdvice: If the side walls are hard and live, a bit of diffusion rather than absorption can help keep the room sounding natural.

okay.text: Moderate reflections. The image is there but it could be tighter — especially on complex mixes.
okay.mateAdvice: Check if the ceiling first-reflection point is treated — it's often overlooked and makes a real difference to clarity.

okay.text: Reflections are audible in the data. The fix is targeted absorption, not wallpapering the room in foam.
okay.mateAdvice: Panels between 5–10cm thick at the side and ceiling reflection points is all you really need here.

good.text: Reflections are well under control. The phantom center should be stable and the imaging convincing.
Good reflection management. The direct sound is clearly dominant — the stereo image will feel solid.
Nice. First reflections aren't overwhelming the direct sound, so the imaging is sitting where it should.
good.mateAdvice: A bit more ceiling treatment is the one remaining thing that could push this from good to excellent.

good.text: The side walls are doing their job. Stereo image should feel natural and well-defined.

excellent.text: Reflections are minimal and the direct sound is fully in charge. The phantom center will be locked in solid.
Seriously good reflection control. The stereo image should be sharp, wide, and stable.
The room gets out of the way on reflections. That's genuinely hard to achieve and you've done it.
Near-anechoic in the early window — outstanding. The imaging will be as precise as your speakers will allow.

poor.text: It's a bit thin, isn't it? You're missing that foundational thump that makes music feel physical rather than just loud.
poor.mateAdvice: A subwoofer crossed over around 80Hz would fill that gap immediately — or try pulling the speakers further into the room first.

poor.text: The low end rolls off early. The bass on the recordings is there; the room just isn't delivering it.
poor.mateAdvice: Small rooms struggle with deep bass extension — a properly integrated sub is often the most practical solution.

poor.text: Sub-bass is either missing or inconsistent. You're not hearing the full bottom of the music.
poor.mateAdvice: Temporarily placing the speakers in the corners will show you how much low end the room can actually support.

poor.text: The usable bandwidth is narrow down low. Kick drum, bass guitar, organ — those instruments will feel absent.
poor.mateAdvice: Before buying a sub, check the speaker spacing — SBIR can rob you of apparent bass extension before you've even started.

okay.text: Bandwidth's limited at the extremes but the mid-range is solid. You're not hearing everything, but you're not missing the main event.
okay.mateAdvice: Some speaker placement work and potentially a sub would fill the picture out considerably.

okay.text: Reasonable extension, but the low end rolls off earlier than you'd want for serious listening.
okay.mateAdvice: Moving the speakers a touch closer to the front wall can sometimes buy back a few Hz of extension — worth a try.

okay.text: The room delivers acceptable bandwidth. Not the last word in deep bass, but not embarrassing either.
okay.mateAdvice: If you're after more bottom end, a sub is the most reliable way to extend the bandwidth in this kind of room.

okay.text: Mid-range bandwidth is good; the low end just needs a bit more support to fill the picture.
okay.mateAdvice: Bass traps won't extend bandwidth but they'll make what's there sound more controlled and believable.

good.text: Good bandwidth. The room delivers solid low-frequency support and you're hearing most of what the recording has to offer.
Strong extension into the low end. The room is working in your favour here.
Broad usable bandwidth. The thump is there, the air is there — solid result all round.
Good low-end reach. You're not missing anything obvious and the room isn't artificially inflating what's there.

excellent.text: Full, extended bandwidth. You're getting the bottom end the recording intended you to hear.
Exceptional bandwidth. Deep extension and even distribution — the room isn't limiting you anywhere.
Outstanding low-frequency reach. Reference-level bandwidth for a domestic room.
The whole frequency range is present and accounted for. From the top of the highs to the depths of the sub-bass, you've got it.

poor.text: Left/right balance is noticeably off. The sweet spot is pulling to one side — you're not in the middle of the stereo image.
poor.mateAdvice: Check that the speakers are equidistant from the side walls. Even 10cm of asymmetry will tilt the whole soundstage.

poor.text: Significant asymmetry in the stereo image. The phantom center is wandering — vocals and lead instruments won't feel anchored.
poor.mateAdvice: Try centering your listening seat relative to the room width first, before you start adjusting speaker position.

poor.text: The tonal balance is lopsided. One channel is being reinforced by the room differently from the other.
poor.mateAdvice: Matching the acoustic treatment on both side walls is often more effective than tweaking speaker positions.

poor.text: Room asymmetry is hurting you here. The left and right channels are not reaching your ears with the same tonal character.
poor.mateAdvice: If the room is genuinely asymmetric, treating the live side with absorption to match the dead side often corrects this.

okay.text: Minor tonal asymmetry — the sweet spot is approximately where it should be but slightly off centre.
okay.mateAdvice: Slide the listening seat about 15–20cm toward the centre of the room width and re-measure.

okay.text: Slight left/right imbalance in the tonal response. Usually a placement issue rather than a treatment issue at this level.
okay.mateAdvice: Measure from each speaker to the nearest side wall — if they're not equal, that's your first fix.

okay.text: Balance is close but not perfect. The stereo image is mostly stable but might feel slightly weighted to one side.
okay.mateAdvice: Try centering the listening position relative to the room and see if that closes the gap before adding any treatment.

okay.text: Some tonal asymmetry is present but it's mild. Placement tweaks first, treatment second.
okay.mateAdvice: Check for large asymmetric objects in the room — a big bookshelf on one side but not the other is a common culprit.

good.text: Good left/right balance. The stereo field is centred and the image should feel stable.
Solid tonal symmetry. The phantom center should sit right between the speakers where it belongs.
Good balance. Both channels are being treated consistently by the room.
Tonal balance is well-maintained. You're in the sweet spot and it's doing its job.

excellent.text: Near-perfect stereo balance. Both channels have the same tonal character — the image will be rock solid.
Excellent symmetry. The phantom center will sit exactly where it should and stay there.
Outstanding balance. This is a genuinely symmetric acoustic environment — rare and genuinely valuable.
Both channels are delivering the same signature. The sweet spot is real and the stereo image has a proper foundation.

poor.text: The response is ragged — serious comb filtering happening here. The parallel walls are bouncing sound back and forth like a pinball machine.
poor.mateAdvice: Break up the parallel surfaces. Even a bookshelf of records on a bare wall is better than nothing for flutter echo.

poor.text: Flutter echo is almost certainly present. Clap your hands — if you hear a metallic ringing decay, that's in your music too.
poor.mateAdvice: Diffusion on the back wall behind the listening position is often the single biggest smoothness fix you can make.

poor.text: Spectral roughness is high. The room is colouring everything it plays with an uneven tonal signature.
poor.mateAdvice: A mix of absorption and diffusion is what you need here — pure absorption can make a room sound unpleasantly dead.

poor.text: Heavily comb-filtered response. The sound is going through a blender before it reaches your ears.
poor.mateAdvice: Heavy curtains, stuffed bookshelves, or some proper 2D diffusers on the rear wall would make an immediate difference.

okay.text: Some spectral roughness — the response has a bit of texture to it. You'll hear it most on dense mixes.
okay.mateAdvice: Diffusion tends to help more than absorption for this kind of problem — keeps the room from going dead.

okay.text: Moderate unevenness in the mid-range. Not full-on flutter echo, but the parallel surfaces are interfering with each other.
okay.mateAdvice: A diffuser panel on the back wall and some soft furnishings generally sort this kind of roughness out.

okay.text: Comb filtering is present but not dominant. A bit of diffusion would tame it without killing the room's natural life.
okay.mateAdvice: Panel diffusers around 10–20cm deep are very effective for this frequency range.

okay.text: The response has some texture. You're adding a bit of colouration to everything you listen to.
okay.mateAdvice: A bookshelf full of LPs on the back wall is the audiophile's secret weapon for this kind of diffusion problem.

good.text: Smooth response overall. No obvious colouration — the room is adding very little of its own character.
Good spectral smoothness. The room's not putting its fingerprints all over the music.
Solid smoothness. Real rooms always have some texture but you're well within normal bounds.
The response is relatively clean. A diffuser on the rear wall is about all that's left here.

excellent.text: Exceptionally smooth response. The room is practically transparent — it's adding almost nothing of its own.
Outstanding spectral smoothness. This is reference-level room behaviour.
The frequency response is clean. Colouration from the room is minimal and what's left is natural.
Near-anechoic smoothness in the mid and high range. Whatever you've done here, it's working.

poor.text: Clarity is suffering. The room's holding onto sound for too long — notes blur into each other like a wet reverb you didn't ask for.
poor.mateAdvice: Bass traps are the priority here — they bring down RT60 in the low end where most of that smearing is coming from.

poor.text: The decay time is too long. Fast transients — snare hits, plucked strings, piano attacks — are getting smeared into mush.
poor.mateAdvice: Absorption spread across multiple surfaces reduces RT60 more effectively than one thick panel on a single wall.

poor.text: Instruments blur into each other. The room is keeping a note alive well after the recording has already moved on.
poor.mateAdvice: Thick broadband absorbers on the rear wall and ceiling at the listening position will bring the decay time down significantly.

poor.text: RT60 is high for a listening room. The room is adding a reverb tail that wasn't on the recording.
poor.mateAdvice: The more surface area you treat, the more you bring down RT60 — distributed treatment works much better than concentrating it in one spot.

okay.text: Clarity is average. The decay is longer than ideal and transients aren't as crisp as they could be.
okay.mateAdvice: Absorption at the rear wall and ceiling — mainly bass traps and broadband panels — would make a noticeable difference to musical clarity.

okay.text: Transients are slightly smeared. Kick drum thwack and finger-pick detail will feel a bit soft and indistinct.
okay.mateAdvice: Bass traps and ceiling panels at the listening position are the two most effective things you can add here.

okay.text: Decay time is longer than ideal. Nothing you'd call reverberant, but the room's adding a slight tail to everything.
okay.mateAdvice: Focus treatment on the back half of the room — that's where the sound converges after bouncing around.

okay.text: Reasonable clarity but the room adds a noticeable tail to transient events. Easily fixable if you want to go there.
okay.mateAdvice: Thick rugs and soft furnishings help as much as acoustic panels for this kind of RT60 reduction.

good.text: Good clarity. Transients come through cleanly and the timing of the music feels accurate.
Solid transient definition. The room's decay is well-controlled and the music feels snappy.
Good clarity performance. You're hearing the fine detail — the pick noise, the breath before the note, the room on the recording.
The room resolves transients well. Timing cues in the music should feel accurate and immediate.

excellent.text: Excellent clarity. The room is fast — transients snap through cleanly and nothing smears.
Outstanding transient resolution. The room is completely out of the way when it comes to timing.
Exceptional clarity. The decay is controlled enough to reveal the fine detail on any recording.
Near-studio clarity. Transient events are reproduced with real precision — this is what all the treatment work is for.
